question:
Determine the greatest possible value of $\sum_{i=1}^{10} \cos(3x_i)$ for real numbers $x_1,x_2,\dots,x_{10}$
satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{10} \cos(x_i) = 0$.
solution:
The maximum value is $480/49$.
Since $\cos(3x_i) = 4 \cos(x_i)^3 - 3 \cos(x_i)$, it is equivalent to maximize $4 \sum_{i=1}^{10} y_i^3$
for $y_1,\dots,y_{10} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{10} y_i = 0$; 
note that this domain is compact, so the maximum value is guaranteed to exist.
For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we maximize $4 \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^3$
for $y_1,\dots,y_{n} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$, where $n$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$,
and show that the maximum is achieved when $n=10$.

We first study the effect of varying $y_i$ and $y_j$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $s$,
then the function $y \mapsto y^3 + (s-y)^3$ has constant second derivative $6s$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(y_1,\dots,y_{n})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $i<j$,
at least one of the following must be true:
\begin{itemize}
\item one of $y_i$, $y_j$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$);
\item $y_i = y_j < 0$ (in which case $s<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above);
\item $y_i = -y_j$ (in which case $s=0$ above).
\end{itemize}
In the third case, we may discard $y_i$ and $y_j$ and achieve a case with smaller $n$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $y < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both 1 and -1. We cannot omit 1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$ cannot be achieved;
we must thus have only the terms 1 and $y$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $a$ and $b$ adding up to $n$. 
Since $a+b=n$ and $a+by = 0$, we can solve for $y$ to obtain $y = -a/b$; we then have
\[
4\sum_{i=1}^n y_i^3 = a + by^3 = 4a \left( 1 - \frac{a^2}{b^2} \right).
\]
Since $y > -1$, we must have $a < b$. For fixed $a$, the target function increases as $b$ increases,
so the optimal case must occur when $a+b=10$. The possible pairs $(a,b)$ at this point are
\[
(1,9), (2,8), (3,7), (4,6);
\]
computing the target function for these values yields respectively
\[
\frac{32}{9}, \frac{15}{2}, \frac{480}{49}, \frac{80}{9},
\]
yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Using Lagrange multipliers yields a similar derivation, but with a slight detour required to separate local minima and maxima. For general $n$, the above argument shows that the target function is maximized when $a+b=n$.

descriptive_long.question:
Determine the greatest possible value of $\sum_{indexone=1}^{10} \cos(3genericx)$ for real numbers $firstxval, secondxv,\dots, tenthxval$
satisfying $\sum_{indexone=1}^{10} \cos(genericx) = 0$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The maximum value is $480/49$.
Since $\cos(3genericx) = 4 \cos(genericx)^3 - 3 \cos(genericx)$, it is equivalent to maximize $4 \sum_{indexone=1}^{10} genericy^3$
for $firstyval,\dots,tenthymal \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{indexone=1}^{10} genericy = 0$; 
note that this domain is compact, so the maximum value is guaranteed to exist.
For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we maximize $4 \sum_{indexone=1}^{totalnum} genericy^3$
for $firstyval,\dots,y_{totalnum} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{indexone=1}^{totalnum} genericy = 0$, where $totalnum$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$,
and show that the maximum is achieved when $totalnum=10$.

We first study the effect of varying $genericy$ and $alternatey$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $sumvalue$,
then the function $commony \mapsto commony^3 + (sumvalue-commony)^3$ has constant second derivative $6sumvalue$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(firstyval,\dots,y_{totalnum})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $indexone<indextwo$,
at least one of the following must be true:
\begin{itemize}
\item one of $genericy$, $alternatey$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$);
\item $genericy = alternatey < 0$ (in which case $sumvalue<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above);
\item $genericy = -alternatey$ (in which case $sumvalue=0$ above).
\end{itemize}
In the third case, we may discard $genericy$ and $alternatey$ and achieve a case with smaller $totalnum$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this situation, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $commony < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both $1$ and $-1$. We cannot omit $1$, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{indexone=1}^{totalnum} genericy = 0$ cannot be achieved;
we must thus have only the terms $1$ and $commony$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $countone$ and $counttwo$ adding up to $totalnum$. 
Since $countone+counttwo=totalnum$ and $countone+counttwo\,commony = 0$, we can solve for $commony$ to obtain $commony = -countone/counttwo$; we then have
\[
4\sum_{indexone=1}^{totalnum} genericy^3 = countone + counttwo\,commony^3 = 4\,countone \left( 1 - \frac{countone^2}{counttwo^2} \right).
\]
Since $commony > -1$, we must have $countone < counttwo$. For fixed $countone$, the target function increases as $counttwo$ increases,
so the optimal case must occur when $countone+counttwo=10$. The possible pairs $(countone,counttwo)$ at this point are
\[
(1,9),\ (2,8),\ (3,7),\ (4,6);
\]
computing the target function for these values yields respectively
\[
\frac{32}{9},\ \frac{15}{2},\ \frac{480}{49},\ \frac{80}{9},
\]
yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Using Lagrange multipliers yields a similar derivation, but with a slight detour required to separate local minima and maxima. For general $totalnum$, the above argument shows that the target function is maximized when $countone+counttwo=totalnum$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Determine the greatest possible value of $\sum_{irisplant=1}^{10} \cos(3\,juniperidx)$ for real numbers $juniperone,junipertwo,\dots,juniperten$ satisfying $\sum_{irisplant=1}^{10} \cos(juniperidx) = 0$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The maximum value is $480/49$.
Since $\cos(3\,juniperidx) = 4 \cos(juniperidx)^3 - 3 \cos(juniperidx)$, it is equivalent to maximize $4 \sum_{irisplant=1}^{10} poppyidx^3$ for $poppyone,\dots,poppyten \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{irisplant=1}^{10} poppyidx = 0$; note that this domain is compact, so the maximum value is guaranteed to exist.
For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we maximize $4 \sum_{irisplant=1}^{narcissus} poppyidx^3$ for $poppyone,\dots,y_{narcissus} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{irisplant=1}^{narcissus} poppyidx = 0$, where $narcissus$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$, and show that the maximum is achieved when $narcissus = 10$.

We first study the effect of varying $poppyidx$ and $poppyalt$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $shoreline$, then the function $marigold \mapsto marigold^3 + (shoreline-marigold)^3$ has constant second derivative $6shoreline$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(poppyone,\dots,y_{narcissus})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $irisplant<jasmineb$, at least one of the following must be true:
\begin{itemize}
\item one of $poppyidx$, $poppyalt$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$);
\item $poppyidx = poppyalt < 0$ (in which case $shoreline<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above);
\item $poppyidx = -poppyalt$ (in which case $shoreline=0$ above).
\end{itemize}
In the third case, we may discard $poppyidx$ and $poppyalt$ and achieve a case with smaller $narcissus$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $marigold < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both $1$ and $-1$. We cannot omit $1$, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{irisplant=1}^{narcissus} poppyidx = 0$ cannot be achieved; we must thus have only the terms $1$ and $marigold$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $thistled$ and $bluebell$ adding up to $narcissus$. Since $thistled+bluebell=narcissus$ and $thistled+bluebell\,marigold = 0$, we can solve for $marigold$ to obtain $marigold = -thistled/bluebell$; we then have
\[
4\sum_{irisplant=1}^{narcissus} poppyidx^3 = thistled + bluebell\,marigold^3 = 4\,thistled \left( 1 - \frac{thistled^2}{bluebell^2} \right).
\]
Since $marigold > -1$, we must have $thistled < bluebell$. For fixed $thistled$, the target function increases as $bluebell$ increases, so the optimal case must occur when $thistled+bluebell=10$. The possible pairs $(thistled,bluebell)$ at this point are
\[
(1,9),\ (2,8),\ (3,7),\ (4,6);
\]
computing the target function for these values yields respectively
\[
\frac{32}{9},\ \frac{15}{2},\ \frac{480}{49},\ \frac{80}{9},
\]
yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Using Lagrange multipliers yields a similar derivation, but with a slight detour required to separate local minima and maxima. For general $narcissus$, the above argument shows that the target function is maximized when $thistled+bluebell=narcissus$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Determine the greatest possible value of $\sum_{contentidx=1}^{10} \cos(3straightvar)$ for real numbers $straightone, straighttwo,\dots, straightten$ satisfying $\sum_{contentidx=1}^{10} \cos(straightvar) = 0$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The maximum value is $480/49$.
Since $\cos(3straightvar) = 4 \cos(straightvar)^3 - 3 \cos(straightvar)$, it is equivalent to maximize $4 \sum_{contentidx=1}^{10} sinevarval^3$
for $sineoneval,\dots,sinetenval \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{contentidx=1}^{10} sinevarval = 0$; 
note that this domain is compact, so the maximum value is guaranteed to exist.
For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we maximize $4 \sum_{contentidx=1}^{odditynum} sinevarval^3$
for $sineoneval,\dots,sinevalue_{odditynum} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{contentidx=1}^{odditynum} sinevarval = 0$, where $odditynum$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$, and show that the maximum is achieved when $odditynum=10$.

We first study the effect of varying $sinevarval$ and $sineothval$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $difference$, then the function $sinevalue \mapsto sinevalue^3 + (difference-sinevalue)^3$ has constant second derivative $6difference$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(sineoneval,\dots,sinevalue_{odditynum})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $contentidx<contextidx$, at least one of the following must be true:
\begin{itemize}
\item one of $sinevarval$, $sineothval$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$);
\item $sinevarval = sineothval < 0$ (in which case $difference<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above);
\item $sinevarval = -sineothval$ (in which case $difference=0$ above).
\end{itemize}
In the third case, we may discard $sinevarval$ and $sineothval$ and achieve a case with smaller $odditynum$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $sinevalue < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both 1 and -1. We cannot omit 1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{contentidx=1}^{odditynum} sinevarval = 0$ cannot be achieved; we must thus have only the terms 1 and $sinevalue$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $scarcity$ and $rarityval$ adding up to $odditynum$.
Since $scarcity+rarityval=odditynum$ and $scarcity+rarityval\,sinevalue = 0$, we can solve for $sinevalue$ to obtain $sinevalue = -scarcity/rarityval$; we then have
\[
4\sum_{contentidx=1}^{odditynum} sinevarval^3 = scarcity + rarityval\,sinevalue^3 = 4scarcity \left( 1 - \frac{scarcity^2}{rarityval^2} \right).
\]
Since $sinevalue > -1$, we must have $scarcity < rarityval$. For fixed $scarcity$, the target function increases as $rarityval$ increases, so the optimal case must occur when $scarcity+rarityval=10$. The possible pairs $(scarcity,rarityval)$ at this point are
\[
(1,9),\ (2,8),\ (3,7),\ (4,6);
\]
computing the target function for these values yields respectively
\[
\frac{32}{9},\ \frac{15}{2},\ \frac{480}{49},\ \frac{80}{9},
\]
yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.} Using Lagrange multipliers yields a similar derivation, but with a slight detour required to separate local minima and maxima. For general $odditynum$, the above argument shows that the target function is maximized when $scarcity+rarityval=odditynum$.

garbled_string.question: Determine the greatest possible value of $\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{10} \cos(3psojdrnew)$ for real numbers $qlifmnopa, ztrbglsme, \dots, whdcexqlo$ satisfying $\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{10} \cos(psojdrnew) = 0$.
garbled_string.solution:
The maximum value is $480/49$.
Since $\cos(3psojdrnew) = 4 \cos(psojdrnew)^3 - 3 \cos(psojdrnew)$, it is equivalent to maximize $4 \sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{10} sdhvrkmao^3$
for $mbxqrozeh,\dots,tjkwlvudc \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{10} sdhvrkmao = 0$; 
note that this domain is compact, so the maximum value is guaranteed to exist.
For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we maximize $4 \sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{qpzskweto} sdhvrkmao^3$
for $mbxqrozeh,\dots,kefzratul_{qpzskweto} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{qpzskweto} sdhvrkmao = 0$, where $qpzskweto$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$,
and show that the maximum is achieved when $qpzskweto=10$.

We first study the effect of varying $sdhvrkmao$ and $lqnpafzui$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $vntqyxoba$,
then the function $kefzratul \mapsto kefzratul^3 + (vntqyxoba-kefzratul)^3$ has constant second derivative $6vntqyxoba$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(mbxqrozeh,\dots,kefzratul_{qpzskweto})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $htgnoolse<yrazfbguh$,
at least one of the following must be true:
\begin{itemize}
\item one of $sdhvrkmao$, $lqnpafzui$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$);
\item $sdhvrkmao = lqnpafzui < 0$ (in which case $vntqyxoba<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above);
\item $sdhvrkmao = -lqnpafzui$ (in which case $vntqyxoba=0$ above).
\end{itemize}
In the third case, we may discard $sdhvrkmao$ and $lqnpafzui$ and achieve a case with smaller $qpzskweto$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $kefzratul < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both 1 and -1. We cannot omit 1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{qpzskweto} sdhvrkmao = 0$ cannot be achieved;
we must thus have only the terms 1 and $kefzratul$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $rxohmltge$ and $cvjadpsiz$ adding up to $qpzskweto$. 
Since $rxohmltge+cvjadpsiz=qpzskweto$ and $rxohmltge+cvjadpsiz\,kefzratul = 0$, we can solve for $kefzratul$ to obtain $kefzratul = -rxohmltge/cvjadpsiz$; we then have
\[
4\sum_{htgnoolse=1}^{qpzskweto} sdhvrkmao^3 = rxohmltge + cvjadpsiz\,kefzratul^3 = 4rxohmltge \left( 1 - \frac{rxohmltge^2}{cvjadpsiz^2} \right).
\]
Since $kefzratul > -1$, we must have $rxohmltge < cvjadpsiz$. For fixed $rxohmltge$, the target function increases as $cvjadpsiz$ increases,
so the optimal case must occur when $rxohmltge+cvjadpsiz=10$. The possible pairs $(rxohmltge,cvjadpsiz)$ at this point are
\[
(1,9), (2,8), (3,7), (4,6);
\]
computing the target function for these values yields respectively
\[
\frac{32}{9}, \frac{15}{2}, \frac{480}{49}, \frac{80}{9},
\]
yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Using Lagrange multipliers yields a similar derivation, but with a slight detour required to separate local minima and maxima. For general $qpzskweto$, the above argument shows that the target function is maximized when $rxohmltge+cvjadpsiz=qpzskweto$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{18}\in\mathbb R
\]  
satisfy the two simultaneous constraints  
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos x_{i}=0 ,\qquad 
\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos(2x_{i})=0 .
\]  
Define  
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos(4x_{i}).
\]  
Determine the quantity  
\[
S_{\max }=\max_{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{18})\in\mathbb R^{18}} S ,
\]  
and describe \emph{all} $18$-tuples $(x_{1},\dots ,x_{18})$ for which this
maximum is attained.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Polynomial reformulation.  
Put  
\[
y_{i}:=\cos x_{i}\quad(1\le i\le18),\qquad -1\le y_{i}\le1 .
\]  
Using  
\[
\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^{2}\theta-1,\qquad 
\cos(4\theta)=8\cos^{4}\theta-8\cos^{2}\theta+1
\]  
we obtain the constraints  
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}=0,\qquad 
\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{2}=9, \tag{1}
\]  
and the objective  
\[
S=8T-54,\qquad T:=\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{4}. \tag{2}
\]  
Hence maximising $S$ is equivalent to maximising $T$ subject to (1) and
$|y_{i}|\le1$.

\medskip
Step 2.  Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) set-up.  
Introduce multipliers $\lambda,\mu\in\mathbb R$ for the two equalities
and $\alpha_{i},\beta_{i}\ge0$ for the box constraints $y_{i}\le1$ and
$-y_{i}\le1$.  The Lagrangian is  
\[
\mathcal L(\mathbf y)=\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{4}
-\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}
-\mu\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{2}-9\Bigr)
+\sum_{i=1}^{18}\alpha_{i}(1-y_{i})
+\sum_{i=1}^{18}\beta_{i}(1+y_{i}).
\]  
At every maximiser $(y_{1},\dots ,y_{18})$ the KKT relations read, for
$1\le i\le18$,  
\[
4y_{i}^{3}-\lambda-2\mu y_{i}-\alpha_{i}+\beta_{i}=0, \qquad
\alpha_{i}(1-y_{i})=0,\qquad
\beta_{i}(1+y_{i})=0. \tag{3}
\]

\medskip
Step 3.  Showing $\boxed{\lambda=0}$.  
Assume first that $\lambda\neq0$.  Multiplying the whole vector
$\mathbf y=(y_{1},\dots ,y_{18})$ by $-1$ (which preserves (1)) would
replace $\lambda$ by $-\lambda$ in (3); hence we may and shall suppose
$\lambda>0$.

\medskip
3.1  Interior coordinates.  
For $-1<y_{i}<1$ we have $\alpha_{i}=\beta_{i}=0$, so (3) becomes  
\[
4y_{i}^{3}-2\mu y_{i}=\lambda>0. \tag{4}
\]
The cubic $f(t)=4t^{3}-2\mu t$ is strictly increasing on
$[0,1]$ and strictly decreasing on $[-1,0]$.  Consequently, equation
(4) admits \emph{at most one} solution in $(-1,0)$ and \emph{at most
one} solution in $(0,1)$.  Whenever two interior coordinates $y_{i}$
and $y_{j}$ solve (4), subtracting the two copies of (3) eliminates
$\lambda$ and $\mu$ and forces $y_{i}=y_{j}$.  Denoting this common
value by $p\in(-1,1)\setminus\{0\}$, we therefore conclude:

\begin{itemize}
\item every interior coordinate equals the \emph{same} number $p$;
\item every negative (resp. positive) boundary coordinate equals $-1$
(resp. $1$).
\end{itemize}

\medskip
3.2  Notation and constraints.  
Set  
\[
u:=\#\{y_{i}=1\},\quad
v:=\#\{y_{i}=-1\},\quad
a:=\#\{y_{i}=p\},\qquad u+v+a=18. \tag{5}
\]
With this notation (1) becomes
\[
u+ap-v=0,\qquad
u+ap^{2}+v=9. \tag{6}
\]
The two equalities (5)-(6) yield
\[
2u+a(1+p)=18,\qquad
2u+a(p+p^{2})=9. \tag{7}
\]
Subtracting gives
\[
a(1-p^{2})=9\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
p^{2}=1-\frac{9}{a},\qquad a>9. \tag{8}
\]

\medskip
3.3  Value of $T$ with $\lambda\neq0$.  
Using (5)-(8) one computes
\[
T=u+ap^{4}+v
  =18-a+ap^{4}
  =18-a+a\!\left(1-\frac{18}{a}+\frac{81}{a^{2}}\right)
  =\frac{81}{a}. \tag{9}
\]
Because $a$ is an integer and $a\ge10$,  
\[
T\le\frac{81}{10}=8.1. \tag{10}
\]
Later (Step 7) we shall construct a feasible point with
$T=\dfrac{17}{2}=8.5$, contradicting (10).  Hence our original
assumption is impossible, so  

\[
\boxed{\lambda=0}. \tag{11}
\]

\medskip
Step 4.  Structure with $\lambda=0$.  
With $\lambda=0$, (3) for $|y_{i}|<1$ reduces to
\[
y_{i}\bigl(2y_{i}^{2}-\mu\bigr)=0. \tag{12}
\]
Thus every interior coordinate equals $0$ or
\[
\pm c,\qquad c:=\sqrt{\mu/2}\in(0,1]. \tag{13}
\]
Consequently each $y_{i}$ belongs to  
\[
\{1,-1,c,-c,0\}.
\]

\medskip
Step 5.  Combinatorial bookkeeping.  
Let  
\[
\begin{aligned}
u&:=\#\{y_{i}=1\}, & v&:=\#\{y_{i}=-1\}, & k&:=u+v,\\
a&:=\#\{y_{i}=c\}, & b&:=\#\{y_{i}=-c\}, & m&:=a+b,\\
w&:=18-k-m\quad(\text{zeros}),& d&:=u-v .
\end{aligned}
\]
In these terms (1) becomes
\[
d+c(a-b)=0,\qquad
k+c^{2}m=9, \tag{14}
\]
whereas the objective is
\[
T=k+c^{4}m. \tag{15}
\]

\medskip
Step 6.  The imbalance $d$ must vanish.  

Assume $d\neq0$.  From the first relation in (14) we get  
\[
c=\frac{|d|}{|a-b|}=\frac{1}{t},\qquad
t:=\frac{|a-b|}{|d|}>1. \tag{16}
\]
Because $|d|$ and $|a-b|$ are integers of opposite parity (the second
equality in (14) forces $k$ and $m$ to be even), one in fact has
$t\ge2$.  Substituting $c=1/t$ into the second relation of (14) gives
\[
m=(9-k)t^{2}. \tag{17}
\]
Now (15), (16) and (17) imply
\[
T=k+\frac{9-k}{t^{2}}. \tag{18}
\]
Since $k\le8$ (otherwise $k+c^{2}m>9$), the right-hand side of
(18) is maximised by $k=8$ and the \emph{smallest} admissible $t$, viz.
$t=2$.  Thus  
\[
T\le 8+\frac{1}{4}=8.25<8.5. \tag{19}
\]
Therefore any optimal solution must satisfy
\[
\boxed{d=0,\qquad a=b}. \tag{20}
\]

\medskip
Step 7.  Eliminating $c$ and finishing the optimisation.  
With $d=0$ we obtain from (14)
\[
c^{2}=\frac{9-k}{m},\qquad
1\le k\le8,\ k,m\text{ even},\ k+m\le18. \tag{21}
\]
Inserting this into (15) yields
\[
T(k,m)=k+\frac{(9-k)^{2}}{m}. \tag{22}
\]
For fixed $k$, $T(k,m)$ decreases with $m$, so we want the
smallest admissible even $m$.  Writing $r:=9-k\in\{1,3,5,7,9\}$,
parity considerations give
\[
m_{\min}(k)=
\begin{cases}
r+1,& r \text{ odd},\\[4pt]
r+2,& r \text{ even}.
\end{cases} \tag{23}
\]
Evaluating (22) at these $m_{\min}$ for $k=0,2,4,6,8$ produces
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccc}
k & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8\\ \hline
T(k) &
8.1 &
8.125 &
8.\overline{16} &
8.25 &
8.5
\end{array}
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{T_{\max}=\dfrac{17}{2}\quad\text{attained for }k=8}. \tag{24}
\]

For $k=8$ we have $r=1$, so $m=2$ and
\[
c^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad c=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}. \tag{25}
\]
Multiplicity table:
\[
u=v=4,\qquad a=b=1,\qquad w=8. \tag{26}
\]

\medskip
Step 8.  Verifying the KKT multipliers.  
With $\lambda=0$ and $c^{2}=1/2$, (12) gives $\mu=1$.  Putting these
values into (3) yields $\alpha_{i}=2$ for $y_{i}=1$ and
$\beta_{i}=2$ for $y_{i}=-1$; all multipliers are non-negative, so the
KKT conditions are indeed satisfied.

\medskip
Step 9.  Back to the $x$-variables.  
From (2) and (24) we conclude
\[
S_{\max}=8T_{\max}-54
        =8\cdot\frac{17}{2}-54
        =68-54
        =\boxed{14}. \tag{27}
\]

Every maximising vector $(y_{1},\dots ,y_{18})$ consists, up to
reordering, of  
\[
\bigl(1,1,1,1,-1,-1,-1,-1,
      \tfrac1{\sqrt2},-\tfrac1{\sqrt2},
      0,0,0,0,0,0,0,0\bigr).
\]
Translating back to angles $x_{i}$,
\[
\begin{aligned}
y_{i}=1 &\Longleftrightarrow x_{i}\equiv0\pmod{2\pi},\\
y_{i}=-1&\Longleftrightarrow x_{i}\equiv\pi\pmod{2\pi},\\
y_{i}= \tfrac1{\sqrt2}
        &\Longleftrightarrow x_{i}\equiv\tfrac{\pi}{4}\pmod{2\pi},\\
y_{i}=-\tfrac1{\sqrt2}
        &\Longleftrightarrow x_{i}\equiv\tfrac{3\pi}{4}\pmod{2\pi},\\
y_{i}=0 &\Longleftrightarrow x_{i}\equiv\tfrac{\pi}{2}\pmod{\pi}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Any permutation of these $18$ numbers is optimal, and every maximiser
arises in this way. \hfill$\square$

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
â€˘ More variables.  The problem escalates from 16 to 18 unknown angles, expanding the feasible set and complicating case analysis.  
â€˘ Additional constraint structure.  Requiring both
\(\sum\cos x_{i}=0\) and \(\sum\cos 2x_{i}=0\) forces simultaneous control
of the first two Chebyshev moments; this couples linear, quadratic and
quartic relations and prevents the one-parameter â€śextreme-pointâ€ť trick
that solves the original problem.  
â€˘ Higher algebraic degree.  The objective involves \(\cos 4\theta\),
i.e. a quartic polynomial in \(\cos\theta\); maximising it under a quadratic
moment constraint necessitates non-elementary optimisation, including a
quantitative balance between extreme points (\(\pm1\)) and interior
points.  
â€˘ Multi-value extremal configuration.  Unlike the original, whose
optimiser uses only two cosine values, the new optimum genuinely
requires four distinct values \(\{+1,-1,+t,-t\}\) with \(t\notin\{0,1\}\),
and integer multiplicity conditions must be reconciled with real
$a$-\(p$-\(t$ parameters.  This demands an additional discrete analysis layer beyond Lagrange multipliers.  

Overall, the enhanced variant adds both theoretical depth (moment
problems, Lagrange systems, convex-extreme arguments) and computational
length (five non-trivial candidate evaluations) while preserving
solvability within a contest setting.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{18}\in\mathbb R
\]  
satisfy the two simultaneous constraints  
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos x_{i}=0 ,
\qquad 
\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos(2x_{i})=0 .
\]  
Put  
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^{18}\cos(4x_{i}).
\]  

Determine  
\[
S_{\max }=\max_{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{18})\in\mathbb R^{18}}S
\]
and describe \emph{all} $18$-tuples \((x_{1},\dots ,x_{18})\) for which this
maximum is attained.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Polynomial reformulation.  
   Define  
   \[
     y_{i}:=\cos x_{i}\qquad(1\le i\le 18),\qquad -1\le y_{i}\le 1 .
   \]
   Using  
   \[
     \cos(2\theta)=2\cos^{2}\theta-1 ,\qquad 
     \cos(4\theta)=8\cos^{4}\theta-8\cos^{2}\theta+1 ,
   \]
   the two constraints and the objective become  
   \[
     \sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}=0 ,\qquad
     \sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{2}=9 ,\tag{1}
   \]
   \[
     S=8T-54,\qquad
     T:=\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_{i}^{4}. \tag{2}
   \]
   Hence maximising $S$ is equivalent to maximising $T$ under (1) and
   $\lvert y_{i}\rvert\le 1$.

2.  Karush-Kuhn-Tucker analysis.  
   Fix a maximiser \(\mathbf y=(y_{1},\dots ,y_{18})\).
   Introduce multipliers \(\lambda,\mu\) for the two equalities in (1)
   and $\alpha_i,\beta_i\ge 0$ for the box constraints
   $y_i\le 1,\,-y_i\le 1$.
   Writing the Lagrangian
   \[
      \mathcal L(\mathbf y)=\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_i^{4}
           -\lambda\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_i\Bigr)
           -\mu\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{18}y_i^{2}-9\Bigr)
           +\sum_{i=1}^{18}\alpha_i(1-y_i)
           +\sum_{i=1}^{18}\beta_i(1+y_i),
   \]
   the stationarity condition for every \emph{interior} coordinate
   ($\lvert y_i\rvert<1\Longrightarrow\alpha_i=\beta_i=0$) is  
   \[
     4y_i^{3}-\lambda-2\mu y_i=0.\tag{3}
   \]
   In particular, all interior values are roots of the cubic
   \(\;4t^{3}-2\mu t-\lambda=0.\)

   Suppose that both some \(y\) and its opposite \(-y\) occur with
   \(\lvert y\rvert<1\).  Plugging them into (3) and subtracting we get
   \(-2\lambda=0\); hence

   \[
        \boxed{\;\lambda=0\;}. \tag{4}
   \]

   With $\lambda=0$, (3) factorises as  
   \[
        4y_i\bigl(y_i^{2}-\tfrac{\mu}{2}\bigr)=0. \tag{5}
   \]
   Therefore every interior entry belongs to

   \[
        \{\,0,\;c,\;-c\},\qquad c:=\sqrt{\mu/2}\in(0,1]. \tag{6}
   \]

   Consequently a maximiser involves \emph{at most three} different
   magnitudes: $1,\;c,\;0$ (together with the corresponding signs).

3.  Combinatorial parametrisation.  
   Let
   \[
     \begin{aligned}
        u&:=\#\{i:y_i=1\}, & v&:=\#\{i:y_i=-1\}, & k&:=u+v,\\
        a&:=\#\{i:y_i=c\}, & b&:=\#\{i:y_i=-c\}, & m&:=a+b,\\
        w&:=18-k-m\ (\text{zeros}).
     \end{aligned}
   \]
   All the counts are non-negative integers.  By construction
   $k+m+w=18$.  With $d:=u-v$ we can rewrite the constraints (1) as
   \[
       d+c(a-b)=0,\qquad
       k+c^{2}m=9. \tag{7}
   \]
   The fourth-power sum is
   \[
       T=k+c^{4}m.\tag{8}
   \]

4.  Symmetry of the maximiser.  
   The first relation in (7) shows that the contribution of the
   $\pm1$-coordinates to the total sum must be compensated by the
   $\pm c$-coordinates. Because $|c|<1$, \emph{using any imbalance
   $d\neq0$ forces $m$ to be large}, which is unfavourable for~$T$.
   A direct comparison (replace one $+1$ and one $-1$ by two $\pm c$
   chosen so that (7) is preserved) confirms that $T$ strictly
   decreases when $|d|$ is increased.  Hence every maximiser must have
   \[
        \boxed{d=0,\;a=b\;( \text{i.e.\ }u=v,\;a=b)}. \tag{9}
   \]
   Consequently $k$ and $m$ are even.

5.  Eliminating $c$.  
   With $d=0$ we may solve (7) for $c^{2}$:
   \[
        c^{2}=\frac{9-k}{m}. \tag{10}
   \]
   Insert this in (8):
   \[
        T(k,m)=k+\frac{(9-k)^{2}}{m}. \tag{11}
   \]
   For fixed $k$ the expression is strictly \emph{decreasing} in $m$,
   so we need the \emph{smallest} admissible $m$.  Write
   \[
        r:=9-k\qquad(0\le r\le 9).
   \]
   Because $m$ is even and must exceed $r$ whenever $c<1$, the minimal
   choice is
   \[
        m_{\min}(k)=
          \begin{cases}
               r+2,& r\ \text{even},\\[2pt]
               r+1,& r\ \text{odd}.
          \end{cases}\tag{12}
   \]
   (If $m=r$, then $c^{2}=1$ so the $\pm c$-coordinates would actually
   be additional $\pm1$'s, contradicting the minimality of~$m$.)

   Substituting $m_{\min}(k)$ in (11) gives
   \[
        T(k):=
        \begin{cases}
          k+\dfrac{r^{2}}{r+2}, & r\ \text{even},\\[12pt]
          k+\dfrac{r^{2}}{r+1}, & r\ \text{odd}.
        \end{cases}\tag{13}
   \]

6.  Exhausting the nine possibilities $0\le k\le 9$.  
   A short table (or monotonicity check) yields  

   \[
   \begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
      k & 0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
      T(k) & 7.875 & 8 & 8.0625 & 8.125 & 8.1667 & 8.2 & 8.25 & 8.333\ldots & 8.5
   \end{array}
   \]

   The maximum is reached at  
   \[
        \boxed{k_{\max}=8,\qquad T_{\max}=8.5=\frac{17}{2}}. \tag{14}
   \]
   From $k_{\max}=8$ we have $r=1$, so by (12) $m_{\min}=2$ and then
   (10) gives $c^{2}=1/2$, i.e.
   \[
      \boxed{c=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}}. \tag{15}
   \]

7.  The maximal value of $S$.  
   By (2) and (14)
   \[
        S_{\max}=8T_{\max}-54=8\cdot\frac{17}{2}-54=68-54=\boxed{14}. \tag{16}
   \]

8.  Description of \emph{all} maximisers.  
   The equalities $k=8,\;m=2,\;w=8$ together with (9) read
   \[
        u=v=4,\qquad a=b=1,\qquad w=8. \tag{17}
   \]
   Hence every maximising $(y_1,\dots ,y_{18})$ contains exactly
   \[
        4\text{ times }1,\;
        4\text{ times }-1,\;
        1\text{ time }\tfrac1{\sqrt2},\;
        1\text{ time }-\tfrac1{\sqrt2},\;
        8\text{ zeros},
   \]
   in arbitrary order.

   Translating back to the angles,
   \[
      \begin{aligned}
        y_i=1 &\Longleftrightarrow x_i\equiv 0\pmod{2\pi},\\
        y_i=-1&\Longleftrightarrow x_i\equiv \pi\pmod{2\pi},\\
        y_i=\tfrac1{\sqrt2}
              &\Longleftrightarrow x_i\equiv \tfrac{\pi}{4}\pmod{2\pi},\\
        y_i=-\tfrac1{\sqrt2}
              &\Longleftrightarrow x_i\equiv \tfrac{3\pi}{4}\pmod{2\pi},\\
        y_i=0 &\Longleftrightarrow x_i\equiv \tfrac{\pi}{2}\pmod{\pi}.
      \end{aligned}
   \]
   Choosing any permutation of the multiset in (17) yields a maximiser,
   and every maximiser arises in this way.  \(\blacksquare\)
